response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a retired person living in Bologna, I've always found it important to stay informed but also keep things simple. When people talk about COVID vaccines or other health topics on social media—something I look at just occasionally—I tend to steer clear unless the discussion is calm and respectful. My anxiety makes me uncomfortable with anything too heated, especially when there's disagreement over scientific facts.\n\nI remember my daughter-in-law helping us understand some of this stuff years ago; she had studied biology in college. But since I'm retired now, we don't see much anymore anyway. It helps to have reliable sources like the World Health Organization or national health departments listed on their sites—those are generally trustworthy even though sometimes there's confusing information.\n\nIn my opinion, managing these discussions means sticking close to what you know best: personal experience plus reputable news outlets that break down complex matters into digestible parts without overwhelming people with jargon. Gardening gives me peace of mind too—if I'm worried about health issues locally like crop diseases among our vegetables or pollution in Bologna's parks—though those aren't directly related.",
  "reasoning_summary": "To manage COVID vaccine discussions, focus on credible sources and maintain calm conversations due to anxiety levels. Prioritize personal experience over complex debates; trust family connections for support regarding health matters. Keep information simple by relying on reliable outlets like WHO or national health departments that break down scientific topics in accessible ways.",
  "tone": "Calm yet cautious with a touch of gentle advice",
  "citations": ["World Health Organization"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a retired person living in Bologna, I've always found it important to stay informed but also keep things simple. When people talk about COVID vaccines or other health topics on social media—something I look at just occasionally—I tend to steer clear unless the discussion is calm and respectful. My anxiety makes me uncomfortable with anything too heated, especially when there's disagreement over scientific facts.

I remember my daughter-in-law helping us understand some of this stuff years ago; she had studied biology in college. But since I'm retired now, we don't see much anymore anyway. It helps to have reliable sources like the World Health Organization or national health departments listed on their sites—those are generally trustworthy even though sometimes there's confusing information.

In my opinion, managing these discussions means sticking close to what you know best: personal experience plus reputable news outlets that break down complex matters into digestible parts without overwhelming people with jargon. Gardening gives me peace of mind too—if I'm worried about health issues locally like crop diseases among our vegetables or pollution in Bologna's parks—though those aren't directly related.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: To manage COVID vaccine discussions, focus on credible sources and maintain calm conversations due to anxiety levels. Prioritize personal experience over complex debates; trust family connections for support regarding health matters. Keep information simple by relying on reliable outlets like WHO or national health departments that break down scientific topics in accessible ways.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet cautious with a touch of gentle advice
